full_response:
We start with flat Minkowski space written in the usual (laboratory) cylindrical coordinates (t , r , θ , z)

ds² = −c² dt² + dr² + r² dθ² + dz² ,                (1)

and we will restrict our attention to the plane z = const (the merry-go-round is a disk).  
Hence

ds² = −c² dt² + dr² + r² dθ² .                     (2)

1. Choice of co-moving (primed) coordinates  
The rim of the merry-go-round is rotating with constant angular velocity ω.  
A point fixed on the rim therefore has

r = R    (constant) ,      θ(t) = θ₀ + ω t .

A coordinate system adapted to these observers is obtained with

t′ = t                 (time measured by the laboratory clocks) ,
r′ = r                 (the instantaneous radial distance) ,
θ′ = θ − ω t = θ − ω t′    (the angle measured in the co-rotating frame).     (3)

(Nothing is done to z, which we have already suppressed.)

2. Differentials of the new coordinates  
From (3)

dt = dt′ ,
dr = dr′ ,
dθ = dθ′ + ω dt′ .                                  (4)

3. Substitute into the line element  
Insert (4) into (2):

ds²
 = −c² dt′² + dr′² + r′² (dθ′ + ω dt′)²
 = −c² dt′² + dr′² + r′² dθ′² + 2 ω r′² dt′ dθ′ + ω² r′² dt′²
 = −(c² − ω² r′²) dt′² + dr′² + r′² dθ′² + 2 ω r′² dt′ dθ′ .    (5)

Equation (5) is the desired line element in the rotating (primed) coordinates.

4. Components of the metric tensor  
Reading off the coefficients of the quadratic form in (5) we get, in the coordinate order (t′ , r′ , θ′),

g′_{t′t′} = −(c² − ω² r′²) ,
g′_{r′r′} = 1 ,
g′_{θ′θ′} = r′² ,
g′_{t′θ′} = g′_{θ′t′} = ω r′² ,                     (6)

all other components vanishing.  Written as a matrix,

g′_{μν} =
⎡ −(c²−ω² r′²)   0          ω r′² ⎤
⎢    0           1             0   ⎥
⎣  ω r′²         0           r′²  ⎦ ,

which is manifestly symmetric under μ ↔ ν, as required.

\boxed{ds^{2}=-(c^{2}-\omega^{2}r^{\prime 2})\,dt^{\prime 2}+dr^{\prime 2}+r^{\prime 2}d\theta^{\prime 2}+2\,\omega r^{\prime 2}\,dt^{\prime}d\theta^{\prime},\qquad
g^{\prime}_{t^{\prime}t^{\prime}}=-(c^{2}-\omega^{2}r^{\prime 2}),\;
g^{\prime}_{r^{\prime}r^{\prime}}=1,\;
g^{\prime}_{\theta^{\prime}\theta^{\prime}}=r^{\prime 2},\;
g^{\prime}_{t^{\prime}\theta^{\prime}}=g^{\prime}_{\theta^{\prime}t^{\prime}}=\omega r^{\prime 2}} 

Confidence: 82%
